bins.name: Automatic UFLS as system protection requirement
bins.desc: Large loads must be evaluated for their interaction with automatic underfrequency load shedding (UFLS) programs, which are the last-line defense against system collapse. UFLS functionality must be assured through proactive design before interconnection, not reactive review after the fact.
quotes: Large loads – whether co-located with generating facilities or standalone – will most likely be interconnected at voltage levels exceeding the 100kV North American Electric Reliability Corporation ("NERC") definition of Bulk Electric System. As a result, this will bring the interconnection of such large load facilities within the scope of the Commission-approved Electric Reliability Organization ("ERO") mandatory requirements that are designed to preserve the reliable operation of the power system.

While there are many areas of reliability related concern, one that has not been raised but is vital is automatic UFLS programs and their role as the last line of defense used during periods of stressed system conditions after operators have exhausted all of their manual load shedding (i.e., rotating blackout) options.
Potential adverse impacts to reliability and resilience must be examined in advance (not reactively) and be addressed through the design of the interconnection facility.

The NYSRC is in support of the Commission's inquiry into necessary reforms to effectuate the interconnection of large loads. Accordingly, the NYSRC submits that automatic UFLS programs must be considered in the design of large load facilities interconnecting to the transmission system from a preemptive or proactive planning perspective.

bins.name: Pre-interconnection reliability and resilience studies
bins.desc: Comprehensive studies of reliability and resilience impacts must be conducted in advance of interconnection and incorporated into facility design. Current five-year UFLS review cycles are inadequate for the pace and magnitude of large load additions.
quotes: Potential adverse impacts to reliability and resilience must be examined in advance (not reactively) and be addressed through the design of the interconnection facility.
Given the magnitude of the proposed loads, these studies should be conducted in advance of interconnection to assure the functionality of automatic underfrequency load shedding programs.

The NYSRC is in support of the Commission's inquiry into necessary reforms to effectuate the interconnection of large loads. Accordingly, the NYSRC submits that automatic UFLS programs must be considered in the design of large load facilities interconnecting to the transmission system from a preemptive or proactive planning perspective.

bins.name: NERC standards did not anticipate load sizes
bins.desc: Original NERC standards and PRC-006 were developed when load growth was slow or nonexistent. Current large load additions (500 to 1500 MW) represent a magnitude and pace not envisioned at the time of standard adoption, increasing the probability that UFLS activation will be needed.
quotes: As the power system becomes more operationally stressed due to the increased addition of large loads coupled with the penetration of intermittent resources, the probability of triggering automatic underfrequency load shedding ("automatic UFLS") programs will likely increase for large loads.

NERC did not envision the magnitude of the single load additions that are being contemplated and studied at this time (i.e., 500, 1,000, 1,500 MW/MVA loads). At the time of the initial adoption of PRC-006, load growth was either relatively slow or non-existent in some areas and there was consensus around the current requirement in R4 to perform a functional review of the effectiveness of the UFLS program only once every five-years.

bins.name: Five-year UFLS review cycle inadequate
bins.desc: The current requirement to review UFLS program effectiveness only once every five years is insufficient to accommodate rapid large load additions. This cycle prevents utilities from incorporating new large loads into UFLS studies in time to meet reliability standards.
quotes: NERC did not envision the magnitude of the single load additions that are being contemplated and studied at this time (i.e., 500, 1,000, 1,500 MW/MVA loads). At the time of the initial adoption of PRC-006, load growth was either relatively slow or non-existent in some areas and there was consensus around the current requirement in R4 to perform a functional review of the effectiveness of the UFLS program only once every five-years.

It is likely that without offering some portion of the newly connected large load to become part of the automatic UFLS program, the utility may not be able to find enough additional load to place under automatic UFLS control to meet the NERC or regional standard requirements (due to the five-year examination period).
